source_paper_title: Sample-Driven Optimal Stopping: From the Secretary Problem to the i.i.d. Prophet Inequality
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Correa_2024, title={Sample-Driven Optimal Stopping: From the Secretary Problem to the i.i.d. Prophet Inequality}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1363}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1363}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Correa, José and Cristi, Andrés and Epstein, Boris and Soto, José A.}, year={2024}, month=feb, pages={441–475} }
context:
Section 5.3, pages 23–24 (discussion around the limit as p → 1 and matroid secretary problem consequences)

An interesting question is whether something similar occurs for other classes of independence systems different than matroids of rank 1.

Proving that any of this problems behaves like the limit of p-DOS as p \to 1 on all matroids may be, in fact, a very difficult task.
title: Characterize limiting competitive ratio of p-sample matroid secretary via i.i.d. analogues
background_latex:
Let \((S,\mathcal I)\) be a matroid (or more generally an independence system) on a finite ground set \(S\). In a sampling-based online selection problem parameterized by \(p\in[0,1)\), each element \(e\in S\) is independently placed into an information set \(H\) with probability \(p\) and otherwise into an online set \(S\setminus H\). An adversary assigns nonnegative weights \(Y(e)\ge 0\) to all elements. The algorithm observes the elements in \(H\) (and their relative ranking by weight), then the online elements arrive in uniformly random order; upon each arrival, the algorithm must irrevocably decide whether to accept the element, maintaining that the accepted set remains independent in \((S,\mathcal I)\).

Let \(S[q]\) denote the random subset obtained by including each element of \(S\) independently with probability \(q\). The online set has the same distribution as \(S[1-p]\). For a fixed weight function \(Y\), define
\[\mathrm{OPT}(\mathcal I,q,Y) := \mathbb E\big[\max\{\sum_{e\in I} Y(e): I\in\mathcal I,\ I\subseteq S[q]\}\big],\]
where the expectation is over the randomness of \(S[q]\).

For a class \(\mathcal C\) of independence systems, define \(\beta_{\mathcal C}(p)\) as the infimum, over \((S,\mathcal I)\in \mathcal C\), of the best competitive ratio achievable by any online algorithm in the above \(p\)-sampling model, where competitiveness is measured against \(\mathrm{OPT}(\mathcal I,1-p,Y)\).

Let \(\mathcal M\) denote the class of all matroids. The matroid secretary conjecture asks whether \(\beta_{\mathcal M}(0)>0\), i.e., whether there exists a constant-competitive algorithm for the matroid secretary problem (the case \(p=0\)). The paper observes that understanding the limit \(L:=\lim_{p\to 1} \beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) and identifying an appropriate full-information/i.i.d.-type analogue whose optimal competitive ratio equals \(L\) would have major consequences.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exists a natural matroid-valued analogue of the single-choice i.i.d. prophet inequality whose optimal competitive ratio equals
\[L := \lim_{p\to 1} \beta_{\mathcal M}(p),\]
where \(\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) is the optimal worst-case competitive ratio for the \(p\)-sample-driven online selection problem on the class \(\mathcal M\) of all matroids (with adversarial nonnegative weights and random arrival order), benchmarked against \(\mathrm{OPT}(\mathcal I,1-p,Y)\).

In particular, decide whether one can prove an equality of the form
\[\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p) = \rho,\]
where \(\rho\) is the optimal competitive ratio of a specified i.i.d.-type matroid online-selection model with known distributions (or another clearly defined probabilistic matroid model), analogous to how \(\lim_{p\to 1}\alpha(p)=\alpha^*\) holds in the rank-1 (single selection) case.
latex:

location_in_paper: Section 5.3, pages 23–24 (discussion around the limit as p → 1 and matroid secretary problem consequences)
open_problem_quote:
An interesting question is whether something similar occurs for other classes of independence systems different than matroids of rank 1.

Proving that any of this problems behaves like the limit of p-DOS as p \to 1 on all matroids may be, in fact, a very difficult task.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is about identifying/proving an equivalence between limiting competitive ratios across different probabilistic models on all matroids, not a finite falsifiable statement on bounded-size instances. Automated search over small matroids may generate hypotheses but cannot refute or confirm a universal limiting equivalence.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can encode candidate matroid online algorithms or reductions and evaluate competitive ratios empirically on families of random/adversarial weight assignments for finite matroids, which provides a computable fitness signal. However, the core question is model-identification and asymptotic equality in the limit p→1 over all matroids, so computational experiments would be suggestive rather than decisive.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Correa--Cristi--Epstein--Soto (2021), the rank-1 (single-choice) \(p\)-sample-driven optimal stopping problem is solved exactly via a limit LP and an optimal-transport structural theorem, yielding an optimal threshold policy and a competitive ratio \(\alpha(p)\) that satisfies \(\lim_{p\to 1}\alpha(p)=\alpha^*\approx 0.745\), matching the classical i.i.d. prophet inequality constant. In Section 5.3 they pose the matroid generalization: define \(\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) for the \(p\)-sample matroid secretary model benchmarked against \(\mathrm{OPT}(\mathcal I,1-p,Y)\), and ask whether \(L=\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) equals the optimal competitive ratio of some natural i.i.d./full-information matroid analogue. They observe that proving such an identification with any known constant-competitive matroid prophet/prophet-secretary/random-assignment model would imply \(L>0\) and, by monotonicity in \(p\), would yield \(\beta_{\mathcal M}(0)>0\), resolving the matroid secretary conjecture.

The forward-citing literature provided does not resolve this limiting-characterization question. The closest conceptual progress is the development of bridges between secretary-type random-order models and prophet-type distributional models under matroid constraints. "Secretary Problems, Prophet Inequalities, and Contention Resolution Schemes" shows that constant-competitive matroid secretary is equivalent (up to constants) to the existence of universal random-order contention resolution schemes, reframing the difficulty in terms of online CRS that work for broad (even correlated) priors. On the prophet-from-samples side, "Single-sample prophet inequalities via greedy-ordered selection" gives constant-factor single-sample prophet inequalities for several matroid families (e.g., transversal matroids) and proves that certain strong single-sample prophet guarantees would imply constant-competitive order-oblivious secretary algorithms, suggesting that extending such results to all matroids may be as hard as matroid secretary.

Other cited works contribute tools on the i.i.d./prophet side (posted-price implementations; equivalences between prophet benchmarks) and sharpen rank-1 sampling limits, but none identifies a matroid-valued i.i.d. analogue whose optimal ratio provably equals \(\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\). Thus the problem remains open; a promising direction is to formulate an i.i.d.-type matroid model that captures the information revealed by a \(p\to 1\) sample (nearly full ordinal information on most elements) and then relate its optimal ratio to universal RO contention resolution or to prophet-secretary/pricing frameworks, while avoiding implications that would immediately settle the matroid secretary conjecture.

sources.title: Secretary Problems, Prophet Inequalities, and Contention Resolution Schemes
sources.theorem_or_result: Main theorem (Theorem 3.1): the matroid secretary conjecture is equivalent (up to constant factors) to the existence of universal online random-order contention resolution schemes for matroids. Concretely, the following are equivalent: (i) there exists a constant-competitive algorithm for matroid secretary; (ii) there exist constants \(0<\beta\le \alpha<1\) such that every matroid admits a \((\beta,\alpha)\)-universal random-order contention resolution scheme (RO-CRS), i.e. \(\beta\)-balanced for all \(\alpha\)-uncontentious distributions; (iii) there exists \(c>0\) such that every matroid admits an RO-CRS that is \((c\alpha,\alpha)\)-universal simultaneously for all \(\alpha\in[0,1]\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to characterize \(L=\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) for the \(p\)-sample matroid secretary model and relate it to a natural i.i.d./full-information analogue (as in rank-1 prophet inequalities). This paper does not analyze \(\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) as a function of \(p\) nor identify \(L\) or an i.i.d.-type matroid model whose optimal ratio equals \(L\). Instead it provides a different equivalence: constant-competitiveness of the (standard, \(p=0\)) matroid secretary problem is equivalent to existence of universal random-order contention resolution for correlated priors. The techniques (reductions through correlated prophet secretary and labeled contention resolution) are conceptually adjacent to the ‘i.i.d./stochastic analogue’ theme, but they do not yield the requested limiting characterization as \(p\to 1\).

sources.title: Single-sample prophet inequalities via greedy-ordered selection
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper develops a greedy-threshold framework for single-sample prophet inequalities (SSPI) and proves improved constant competitive ratios for several combinatorial constraints, including: - General (non-bipartite) matching with adversarial edge arrivals: a 16-competitive SSPI (Theorem 2.1). - Bipartite matching with adversarial vertex arrivals: an 8-competitive SSPI (Theorem 3.1) and a truthful 16-competitive mechanism (Theorem 3.2). - Transversal matroids: an 8-competitive SSPI (Theorem 3.3). - Budget-additive combinatorial auctions: a 24-competitive SSPI (Theorem 4.1). - Matroids with the \(\alpha\)-partition property: a \(2\alpha\)-competitive SSPI (Theorem 5.1). It also proves a partial converse between SSPIs and order-oblivious secretary algorithms: any \(\alpha\)-competitive pointwise-SSPI (P-SSPI) implies a \(2\alpha\)-competitive order-oblivious secretary (OOS) algorithm (Theorem 6.2).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks to characterize \(L=\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) for the \(p\)-sample matroid secretary model and identify a natural i.i.d./full-information analogue whose optimal competitive ratio equals \(L\). This paper does not analyze the \(p\)-sample matroid secretary model nor the limit \(p\to 1\), and it does not propose or prove an equality \(\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)=\rho\) for a matroid i.i.d.-type model.

However, it is relevant in two ways:
- It advances the ‘i.i.d./sample-based’ side by giving constant-factor SSPIs for certain matroid classes (transversal matroids; more generally matroids with an \(\alpha\)-partition property), which are probabilistic analogues of prophet inequalities with limited information.
- It clarifies limitations: for a broad class of SSPI policies (P-SSPIs), constant-factor SSPI would imply constant-factor order-oblivious secretary (Theorem 6.2). This suggests that proving strong SSPI results for general matroids (and thus potentially relating to \(L\)) may be as hard as the matroid secretary conjecture unless one uses non-P-SSPI techniques.

So the paper provides partial progress/insight toward the landscape connecting sampling-based online selection and prophet/secretary models, but it does not resolve the specific limiting-ratio characterization problem posed.

sources.title: Power of posted-price mechanisms for prophet inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result: Main result (Theorem 1 / Theorem 4): for any prophet-inequality (Bayesian online optimization) algorithm \(A_{\mathrm{inp}}\) under an arbitrary feasibility constraint \(F\subseteq X_1\times\cdots\times X_n\), there exists a posted-price (pricing-based) algorithm \(A_{\mathrm{out}}\) such that (i) \(A_{\mathrm{out}}\) and \(A_{\mathrm{inp}}\) induce the same joint distribution over assigned outcomes \((A_1,\dots,A_n)\), and (ii) \(\mathbb E[v(A_{\mathrm{out}})]\ge \mathbb E[v(A_{\mathrm{inp}})]\) (indeed agent-wise), while maintaining feasibility. An efficient sampling-based implementation (Theorem 19) achieves approximate preservation and welfare up to \((1-\varepsilon)\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to characterize \(L=\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) for the \(p\)-sample matroid secretary model with adversarial weights, and to identify a natural i.i.d./full-information matroid analogue whose optimal competitive ratio equals \(L\). This paper is about prophet inequalities (Bayesian/i.i.d. values with known distributions) and shows any such algorithm can be implemented via posted prices, extending also to prophet secretary. It does not analyze the \(p\)-sample matroid secretary model, does not define or identify an i.i.d. matroid model matching \(\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\), and provides no results about the limit \(p\to 1\) or about adversarial weights. Its relevance is indirect: it strengthens the toolkit on the i.i.d./prophet side (pricing implementations), which could be useful if one first establishes an equivalence between the \(p\to 1\) sampling model and a prophet-type matroid model.

sources.title: Prophet inequalities via the expected competitive ratio
sources.theorem_or_result: For any downward-closed feasibility family \(\mathcal F\) (including matroids), the worst-case optimal guarantees under the two prophet-inequality objectives RoE and EoR are within constant factors: \(\mathrm{EoR}(\mathcal F)\ge \mathrm{RoE}(\mathcal F)/12\) (Theorem 2) and for every product distribution \(\mathcal D\), \(\mathrm{RoE}(\mathcal F,\mathcal D)\ge \mathrm{EoR}(\mathcal F,\mathcal D)/68\) (Theorem 3), hence \(\mathrm{RoE}(\mathcal F)/\mathrm{EoR}(\mathcal F)\in \Theta(1)\) (Corollary 2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to characterize \(L=\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) for the \(p\)-sample matroid secretary model and to identify a natural i.i.d.-type matroid analogue whose optimal competitive ratio equals \(L\). This paper does not study the \(p\)-sample secretary model nor the \(p\to 1\) limit, and it does not propose an i.i.d. matroid model intended to match \(\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\). Instead it develops constant-factor reductions between two performance measures (ratio of expectations vs expected ratio) for prophet inequalities under downward-closed constraints (including matroids). These techniques (tail/core split; concentration via self-bounding of the offline optimum) could be useful if one tries to relate different benchmarks in any candidate i.i.d.-matroid analogue, but they do not identify \(\rho\) or prove an equality with \(L\).

sources.title: Secretary problems: The power of a single sample
sources.theorem_or_result:
Studies the rank-1 (single-choice) single-sample secretary / two-sided Game of Googol model. Main quantitative results:
- Random-order: a comparison-based “block-rank” algorithm selects the maximum with probability at least 
\[0.5009,\]
and no comparison-based algorithm can exceed 
\[\gamma\approx 0.5024.\]
- Under a no-\(\varepsilon\)-superstars condition, achieves \(\gamma-O(1/\log(1/\varepsilon))\).
- Adversarial-order: optimal success probability equals \(1/4\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a characterization of \(L=\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) for matroid (general-constraint) \(p\)-sample secretary, via an i.i.d./full-information analogue whose optimal competitive ratio equals \(L\). This citing paper does not study matroids or \(p\)-sample matroid secretary; it focuses on the rank-1 “best-choice” objective with one sample per distribution (equivalently, a 1/2 prior-observation model) and proves tight constants (~0.5024 in random order, 1/4 in adversarial order). Its relevance is indirect: it refines understanding of the rank-1 limiting constant in a closely related sampling-information model, and develops comparison-based thresholding/monotonicity and extremal lemmas about cross-rank probabilities \(\Pr[X_i>Y_j]\) that could inspire analogous analyses for more complex constraints. However, it neither defines nor analyzes a matroid-valued i.i.d. analogue, nor connects its constants to \(\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\).

sources.title: Prophet Inequality from Samples: Is the More the Merrier?
sources.theorem_or_result: In the single-choice prophet inequality from samples with unknown independent distributions and an identity-blind pool of samples (k samples per distribution), the paper proves: - Hardness: there exist k0 and c<1/2 such that for all k≥k0 and any online algorithm, the worst-case competitive ratio \(\alpha_k\le c\) (Theorem 3.1, with an explicit constant \(c\approx 0.4997\)). - For ordinal static-threshold algorithms that pick the \(\ell\)-th highest sample as a threshold and accept the first online value exceeding it, the asymptotically optimal competitive ratio as \(k\to\infty\) is \(1-\rho\approx 0.433\), where \(\rho\in(0,1)\) solves \(\rho e^{\rho}=1\) (Theorems 4.1–4.2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to characterize \(L=\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) for p-sample matroid secretary and identify a natural i.i.d./full-information matroid analogue whose optimal ratio equals \(L\). This citing paper does not study matroid secretary or matroid feasibility constraints; it studies rank-1 (single-choice) prophet inequality from samples with unknown distributions and identity-blind samples. Thus it does not determine \(L\) nor propose/verify a matroid-valued i.i.d. analogue matching \(\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\). Indirectly, it provides techniques (total-variation/convex-combination indistinguishability arguments; stochastic-domination analysis of sample-based thresholds) that could be relevant when analyzing sample-driven limits and i.i.d.-type analogues, but only in the rank-1 setting.

sources.title: How to Sell Online (Fast) via Pricing-Based Algorithms
sources.theorem_or_result: This dissertation develops pricing-based algorithms for prophet inequalities (including matroid prophet inequalities with tight factor \(1/2\)), mechanisms for two-sided markets, and a secretary variant with predicted additive gap; it does not analyze the \(p\)-sample matroid secretary model nor characterize \(\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) or an i.i.d.-type matroid analogue matching that limit.
sources.connection: The open problem asks to characterize the limiting competitive ratio of the \(p\)-sample matroid secretary problem as \(p\to 1\) and relate it to a natural i.i.d./prophet-type matroid model. The dissertation contains background on matroid prophet inequalities (full-information stochastic model) and secretary problems, and cites work on interpolations between prophet and secretary models, but it does not define \(\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\), study the \(p\)-sample model, or prove any limit/equality connecting \(\lim_{p\to 1}\beta_{\mathcal M}(p)\) to an i.i.d.-matroid benchmark.
